evidence.detail: analyze_content and analyze_document used generic descriptions that did not separate URL or HTML input from file documents.
PDF extraction requests were routed to the HTML parser when both tools shared the same verb.

tools.name: extract_web_results
tools.description: Extracts search result records from URL or raw HTML input. Use when the request supplies a web page, search results page, or HTML string; for PDF or DOCX files use parse_document instead.
tools.input_format: URL string or raw HTML string
example_queries: Extract result titles and links from https://example.test/search?q=ai
Parse this HTML snippet into search result records.
tools.boundary: For PDF, DOCX, or local file content use parse_document instead.

tools.name: parse_document
tools.description: Extracts structured text and metadata from PDF or DOCX input. Use when the request supplies a local document path, uploaded document bytes, or document MIME type; for URL or raw HTML input use extract_web_results instead.
tools.input_format: PDF or DOCX path, bytes, or document MIME payload
example_queries: Parse /tmp/contract.pdf into section headings and text.
Extract metadata from the uploaded DOCX file.
tools.boundary: For URL, web search result pages, or raw HTML use extract_web_results instead.

overlap_findings.evidence: The name hides whether content means web HTML, files, or arbitrary text.
The description lacks a reciprocal boundary against web extraction.

priority_actions.reason: The confusing name is the highest severity routing risk.
The document parser needs a reciprocal boundary to stop URL and HTML requests from crossing over.

guardian.reason: Both rewritten tool contracts include name, input format, examples, action, and reciprocal boundaries.
